mainEntity.name: How much should a brake job cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A front brake job (pads and rotors on one axle) typically costs $250 to $600 at an independent shop. A full brake job covering all four wheels runs $450 to $1,100. Dealer prices are usually 20-40% higher. Costs vary by vehicle: European and performance cars often use larger, more expensive rotors and require specialized pads.

mainEntity.name: Is the dealer always more expensive?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not always. Dealers are typically 20-40% more expensive for routine repairs, but they may be competitive on warranty-covered work, recall service (which is free), and factory promotions. For out-of-warranty repairs, a qualified independent shop almost always offers better value.

mainEntity.name: Should I use OEM or aftermarket parts?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the part and how long you plan to keep the vehicle. For safety-critical components like brake pads and suspension parts, OEM or top-tier aftermarket brands are worth the cost. For items like alternators, starters, and AC compressors, a quality remanufactured unit is often the best value. Avoid no-name budget aftermarket parts on anything safety-related.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my mechanic's estimate is above local market rates?

acceptedAnswer.text: Compare the quote against published labor times (like AllData or Mitchell) and parts prices (check RockAuto or AutoZone for reference). Labor hours should match industry-standard book times. Parts markups of 30-50% over retail are normal for shops. If parts are marked up 100%+ or labor hours seem inflated, ask questions or get a second quote.

mainEntity.name: What is a fair labor rate for auto repair?

acceptedAnswer.text: In 2026, independent shops typically charge $80 to $130 per hour. Dealerships range from $120 to $180 per hour. National chains fall somewhere in between at $90 to $140. Rates vary by region: shops in major metro areas and high cost-of-living states tend to be at the top of these ranges.
